segments.text: Media standing everywhere
Oh, yeah. So we had to go and tell them off before.
I know, but I told them that they're wasting their time.
Are you all right?
I was going to say, are they leaving?
What did I do?
Have you thrown your toys?
No, I've got another.
You've got another sleeve.
It's got to be there.
Yeah. Okay.
Thanks, Steve.
Okay.
I'm going to go. I just have
to go to
a different committee.
Right. Good morning, Minister. Great to have you here.
Thank you. Thank you so much.
You're welcome. Fire away.
No, you first. So you want to hear some comments from me and then questions?
Yes, please. Any day.
All right. Well, thanks so much. Well, colleagues, thank you very much for the
invite in. Can I recognize, I think all of us were at the field days last week, and
what a celebration of the exceptional nature of New Zealand
agriculture. The interesting thing about the field days, it's not only
about the farmers and their animals or what they grow, but I think it increasingly
becomes a fantastic demonstration of the innovation
that sits behind the New Zealand primary sector.
And so, it was good to see so many farmers and growers out
enjoying themselves and in a better place than they have been
previously.
You all right? Did you just go round there and come back?
Just conflicts because we've got Jones next door and
Ozzy in there. Good.
Welcome back.
All righty. So a couple of things I'll touch on and then, of course, we've got
plenty of time for comments and questions.
First is we released last week the situation out for the primary industries.
There's the forecast for the next year, and the primary sector continues to
deliver for the New Zealand economy.
Expected
exports up 6% to hit $64 billion for the very
first time. Estimated to grow to $70 billion by 2030.
I think it'll actually be higher than that, but it's just positive news and all in
the right direction. The standouts I think were dairy up 5%, a
record of $28.6 billion worth of exports.
Meat and wool up 14%, which is extremely good news
for beef and sheep farmers. A
record $14.1 billion worth of exports.
And hort up 7%, 9.5 billion. Kiwifruit, the standout I
think within that is 16%. The kiwifruit sector is getting great
returns all around the world, almost every market, whilst at
the same time they're increasing their production.
Processed food was up 5%, 3.5 billion, which of course is where there's additional
value being added. The other thing I want
to mention is the state of the sector and the
men and women that work in the primary industry.
When we came to government,
confidence levels in agriculture through public surveys were as
low as -66%. In July of last year, they were
+33%, which is a significant increase.
It's a combination of things, and you've got to remember that we've had
adverse weather events, which is challenging for farmers and growers.
But I think it is on the back of farmers feeling valued,
working hard. We've worked hard to simplify rules, to make it easier
for them to meet their obligations, because they do have to meet obligations.
But at the same time, they're getting better returns in international markets.
From a trade point of view, without good trade access, our farmers and growers
wouldn't be able to produce all that they do.
83% of all of our goods exports come from the primary sector.
More than 15% of GDP is from food and fiber.
Some standouts are now the UK FTA is now three
years in operation. We've seen almost a 79%
increase in exports to the UK during that period of
time, with the primary sector doing a lot of the heavy lifting.
The EU FTA is now two years in force, with an extra $3
billion worth of exports, again, predominantly from the primary sector.
And a few other areas of interest, the UAE FTA, which
on entry to force warrants 99% liberalization, saw a
34% increase in exports in the first six months.
That's been moderated as a result of war in the Middle East, but there's still a
very strong demand. You may well have noticed tariff
rates from the
US, and that has been a confusing and
changing
sort of story. We started at 10% on liberation day,
went up to 15% as a result of us having a very
modest surplus.
That was found to be illegal. It went back to 10%, and the US has just announced
that it's likely to go to 12.5%, which in itself is less than
most exporters thought. They expect it may go back to 15%.
But even with all of that, we've been able to get our beef,
kiwifruit, and a few other things back down to the equivalent of a zero tariff
rate. And our exports to the US have gone up by about 4% by
value over that period of time
We now have greater market access
and demand for what we produce from the primary sector, which is, I
suppose, a good position to be in because our exporters have
options as to where they put their product.
But we face the challenge of how we meet that growing demand for the high
quality and safe food and fiber we produce in New Zealand.
Last week at the field days, we announced a new scheme and
policy called Land Use Flexibility, helping Kiwi farmers and
growers to feed the world. And as we have more market
access, the free trade agreement with India is an example, which has the
potential to be as important to New Zealand's economy and our
primary sector as China is, how can we
increase our production whilst keeping that very high quality for
safe food that are environmentally consciousness.
And so ultimately,
consumers around the world
like the products that come from New Zealand.
They like the story that backs that up, but ultimately they have
choices. So this land use flexibility says to farmers that
actually the government is going to co-invest in projects to
test out how we can improve environmental and climate outcomes
whilst increasing production. And, behind that, we
announced six projects which are co-funded in the private sector, in
dairy, red meat, forestry, in aquaculture,
in horticulture, and around Maori growers.
As an example, in dairy, the project aims to reduce nitrate
leaching by 20% whilst increasing
efficiency and productivity on farm.
On red meat sector, there are pilots will be underway, where the
use of collars
aims to increase after utilization by 20%,
thus creating more red meat with the same
amount of land and so on. I would say that
actually we have an opportunity to feed the world and grow the New Zealand economy.
It cannot be at the detriment of the environment or climate
change. The two have to go together.
We have the ability, as we've seen at the field day, to continue to innovate and
the government with the private sector see this as a
partnership. Finally, in this area around catchment groups, which
again looks at the opportunity around
better management of resources and water.
We've announced $40.5 million over the next four years, an increase of $4.5 million
this year. We support about 51 catchment groups at the moment, well, actually 51,
and we're looking to establish 17 and support 17 collectives to sit over the
top. I think where farmers join together in these catchment groups with
decision makers, you get a much, much better outcome, and we're looking
to encourage that. The budget itself, which is before you,
budget this year allocated $4.77
billion over four years to the Ministry of Primary Industries.
We've found savings of, we announced savings of $13.2
million per year, which we've been able to do.
We've also had an increase of $119 million around aquaculture
settlements, $170 million for wilding
pines, a small $2.2 million to help the
ministry get ready for the India FTA implementation,
and $47 million for other settlements.
Two final bits I'll touch on, and I'm sure we can talk more
in questions. You will be aware that
McCain's has announced the closure of a plant in the Hawke's
Bay, which has created concern. I've been to visit the
region and met with the mayors on a number of occasions to have conversations.
There are a group of vegetable growers there who are exploring whether
a private purchase or cooperative may work in the place of
McCain's exiting. We've committed
$50,000 to help with the early stages of feasibility study to see
whether that business model could be put together.
The growers themselves, with the council and others, are currently working through
that. I would caution that I think it's very, very difficult, but the government
views it, we have a role to play to support those growers
in the meantime to see where they get to.
I'm going to be very cautious in what I would say around that because there are
commercial discussions and negotiations going on at the moment, and I certainly
don't want to get into the middle of those.
But they have made a reasonable case as to
their view that they think there could be a future for a
plant there in the Hawke's Bay, and so we're happy to join with them, to work
through what that could look like.
But I would also caution it's very, very early days.
Finally, on Maori agribusiness, we in the budget have money
allocated to continue to support Maori
enterprises. One of the very big challenges I think we do have is around
underutilized Maori or multiple iwi land.
It's why Mike Buttrick is an associate minister.
I've given him responsibility for Maori agriculture with a
particular focus on working with landowners to try and find ways
to free up land and get better access to that.
Ultimately, about 10% of New Zealand
agricultural land is Maori owned.
It contributes about $12 billion directly in output and employs a lot of
New Zealanders, including a lot of young Maori New Zealanders.
So you'll see a greater focus on land utilization from the government
with Mike's
giving this particular specific responsibility.
We hope to see other things that we can do as we move
forward to help with better utilization of iwi land.
With that in mind, I've got pages more, but why don't I stop for a while?
Fantastic. Right. Well, I know we've got a question from Steve.
Thank you, Madam Chair. And, just a quick note, I appreciate your
finding 50K to support the bid for a growers' cooperative
for McCain.
I think they probably need to find a bit more than that, but it's a step in the
right direction.
As to your expenditure you referred to on-
The deal that was done and announced at Fieldays around the $18 million
contributing to the Dairy New Zealand project around
improving outcomes.
One question I've got for you is that given how well dairy is doing,
aren't they wealthy enough and big enough
to be investing in that stuff themselves?
It's a good project, but why is there $18 million of public money
going towards that
when we're losing arable farmers in Canterbury?
And shouldn't the arable sector be being better supported
through public money?
Well, I'm not going to get into a debate about those you think are wealthy and what
they should be paying. I think overall, the government has funding available and
good projects come forward that will help us meet environmental
obligations or grow the economy, which is good for all New Zealanders.
We've considered them. We don't want to be a government that
necessarily sits there and says, "You're too wealthy, let's take off
you," because that would end up with tax-
No, but you're putting public money.
Yeah.
You're investing-
Let me finish. I didn't interrupt you.
So we can either have question answer or have a debate.
We should do that in Parliament, right? So ultimately, you are right about arable.
So the other reason why I've asked Mike Butcher to take on
responsibility is the allegation for arable farming.
They are finding it more challenging.
There are a range of reasons for that at the moment.
The first is prices around the world are down because of
oversupply,
firstly. Secondly, they face
challenges down in Canterbury, particularly because of a number of weather events.
And then thirdly, ultimately, they have had costs put on them.
Some are to do with input costs from overseas, others are to do with very,
very restrictive rules
in Canterbury around what they can do and how they can do it upon their land.
You'll know that through the RMA and other areas, we're working through to simplify
rules and make them more user-friendly whilst
ensuring environmental protection.
But there is support that goes into arable farming, and it is a priority.
Why have we done that for dairy? Well, the dairy, you're right, is a significant
contributor to the New Zealand economy.
It's a very important part of our export profile.
Although only one part of our export profile, we think that
we can get the environmental gains more quickly,
whilst improving
production by partnering with the sector, Steve,
not telling them we think they are wealthy and therefore they should pay
themselves.
Can I do a follow-up?
Yes, sure.
In terms of the ambition around
reducing nitrate, which is a good ambition.
Mm-hmm.
How do you contend with the fact that we're getting an expansion of
dairy now in Canterbury, looking at 40,000 new cows,
and the primary problem with nitrate leachate is the sheer
volume of nutrient going into catchments.
So
aren't you failing to appropriately
manage that volume of nutrient
when you're allowing further expansion of an already
very extensive,
very intensive form of farming?
So the two are not linked, by the way.
This is a new policy going forward. The other is existing policy.
So land use flexibility is about saying to farmers, rather
than a heavy-handed approach through regulation and councils as to what you can
and can't do with your land. If you can show an improved environmental
outcome, then actually you should have flexibility to look for the better
value from your land. Let me give you an example, which I think
comes from Canterbury, but I'd need to check to be entirely sure.
I went to, visited a group of farms a while ago, a dairy
farm that farmer had the other side of the road,
a beef lot. In winter, he was not allowed to put his
dairy cows there, even though there would be a better environmental outcome because
his soil was better, because it would be viewed as a land use change with the
potential for dairy. So, the rules were getting in the way in a better
environmental outcome. So land use flexibility is about saying, "Actually, we
want better rules. We want an improvement when it comes to the
environment." But the way to do that is technology and
innovation, not closing down farms.
Your point about Canterbury is not an unreasonable one,
but ultimately, that's one of the reasons that we're going through
replacement of
the RMA, because we want better certainty for everybody
involved. And by the way, some of those arable farmers that are finding it very,
very hard to make money on their land are looking for other types of land use,
some of which is likely to be dairy.
Exactly. Yeah. Thank you.
Joy, you had a question.
Thank you, Minister.
I wanted to ask you a question with regard to the baseline
savings
of 13.2 million
per annum over a period of years.
Can you tell us exactly
how you're going to, or the ministry is going to achieve that reduction, and what
specific activities or functions will be cut or lost as a result of that?
Yeah, sure. I'll pass to Ray Smith in a moment, who may be going to go into a bit
more detail. But we're very clear in looking for these savings,
that
I've been very clear and very insistent that actually
there should be no cuts and reduction of service at the front line.
And that's very similar to the process we went through two years ago as
well. And so ultimately, as with many New Zealanders
and many, many farmers who have to look at ways to drive
efficiencies within our organization.
They are doing it every day within their business.
How can we achieve more with the same or a reduced amount
of effort or input? How can we be much more efficient?
But I want to be very clear again, I've asked the department to
ensure that the delivery of frontline services remains the same.
And actually, over the last two years, I think we've seen a significant increase
in effort and activity, both through organizational structure, but
also necessity as weather events and other things have happened in
biosecurity from MPI. But Ray, maybe you want to go in a bit
more detail.
Yeah, sure.
Just first thing says there's no change to frontline.
Service. And if you think about the 13 million, just think about it in the context
of $1.3 billion budget. So, to
quite honestly, these are largely going to be through administrative savings that
we can make through the things we can do in the back office areas or
around the contracts we have in here and with IT systems or
administrative things that we operate.
So,
our staff won't really notice any difference as we go through
this. Whether it's getting a gain on a property
lease or whatever it is that we're trying to do.
At this level, that level of savings
is not going to impact any of the frontline services.
So I'm very confident about that. In fact, we haven't initiated any sort of
reorganizing anything on the basis of that number.
So, sorry, just a minute. So, the second
part to my question was around what specific activities and functions will be
cut. I mean, aside from just-
Office space house
... okay, reduced, sorry, I should say.
So, I see that a large chunk of the reduction is in the
policy advice and ministerial servicing.
So how
will you ensure that OIA responses and WPQ responses
are received on time? And I'd be really interested to know if you'd share what your
rate of on time responses are.
Yeah, they've been very good. It's something I've said to the ministry,
irrespective of the number of parliamentary questions you may
get and our view of how helpful they may be or
OIAs, the rules are clear, the law is the law, so we work
to adhere to those. There are occasions where we miss deadlines, and there'd be a
range of reasons for that. But we have a view
of getting OIAs out as quickly as we can.
And I've got to thank the ministry because we get a very significant number, and
they have a great team that works upon that.
But again, there are many ways where you can draw efficiencies
within many offices, including mine.
We've looked at the processes we have in place, how many people are involved in
that, how we can get decisions more quickly as to information needed and OIAs
going out and so on.
But Ray, I don't know if there's more you want to add to that.
Yeah, it is a big overhead cost
for government agencies because the number of OIAs and parliamentary
questions continues to increase.
Mm-hmm.
And at quite a rapid rate, I have to say.
Mm.
And so we have absorbed a lot there.
I'm not concerned about our ability to do it.
I think we sit around about the 95% mark on OIAs.
But perhaps more important statistic in there or one to look alongside,
if you sort of, as I'm trying to gauge how we're going,
is the complaints that are made to the Ombudsman and how many of those are upheld.
And I think you'll note that we have, I think, only one or two out of the, and
the number of complaints come down.
So even if we sometimes kind of miss that first deadline, we make sure
that we do what complainants or requesters are asking, try to get that
right. On the PG side, I think we're pretty good.
But there are large volumes, and
all of us in the public service are going to have to think about as we go forward.
We can't have an ever-increasing number of these things without some
rethinking about how we deal with responding to them.
Yeah. Can I have that one sec? Yep, thank you.
How many staff positions will be affected?
Well, none-
From the 13.2 million reduction?
None specifically from the 13 point.
We've
currently got 3,467 staff, I think as at the end of May on board.
That's roughly what we had last year.
Our projection was always that that will fall slightly.
So that's now bottom-team forecast
as we find administrative efficiencies.
So we're not targeting any particular areas to take people out of, but we
have been operating
slightly below what we could afford to operate with, with staffing numbers,
and anticipation of the fact that we would have to continue to make savings.
There has been no problem with that in terms of the services we provided.
So I feel that we had planned
forward well on anticipating the fact that we'd have to make these
savings, and so we're in quite a good position.
So you won't see us heading off on a major restructuring to try and achieve the
savings you want.
We might go to it. There's a lot- I've got more subs if I can come back.
Oh, no, we have one more, and then we'll go to some others.
Is it a sub or a new primary? No, it's around jobs
and things. Mm. Is the
ministry's Biosecurity New
Zealand business unit exempt from public service number
job cuts?
No.
It's not.
Well, nothing is exempt other than that which has been stated by the
government, which is there are a range of departments that are in different
categories. I think that was part of the original announcement.
But
the thing to think about when you think about NPI's business
is all of those frontline border security roles are
not funded through taxpayer funds.
Mm-hmm.
They're funded through levies that are collected through passengers or through
cargo.
And so, in a sense, they're not part of that
scheme. So when we think about frontline staff
there, and that's about 700 odd frontline staff work in those frontline
border roles. They are funded through a different mechanism.
And we're happy with the funding that was there because we just reduced the fees
from sort of $16 to $12, I think, on the airport
passenger levy.
Likewise, food safety is about 70 or 80% cost recovered.
So again, businesses pay for the services they get, and that's how the staff are
paid for.
So in terms of biosecurity overall,
I'm not concerned about any of those frontline areas.
But biosecurity,
like all of the other business groups in NPI, all have
functions in the head office with me here in Wellington as well.
And that's where collectively we can probably make more gains
So that comes to the point where I said very clear that in looking for these
efficiencies, actually, the frontline service should not be
affected. But there is a difference between what happens at the back office,
and then those who are out on the frontline.
So ultimately, if you go back to a period of time,
I think under the previous government, we saw a very large increase
in the number of people across the civil service, but not
an equal amount by way of percentage on the frontline.
We've been very focused in this direction that says where efficiency be
made, they should be back of office.
Frontline, actually, where we're able to or where there's necessity, as you've seen
from time to time when there's been a pest infiltration or something, the
resources and people are there to deal with that.
Just seeking quick clarification.
Very quick.
So you're saying they're not exempt from the government's public service broad
number cuts?
So I'll answer this. So you're looking for a headline that says, "Ah, they're going
No, I'm not. I'm simply asking a question, Minister.
So ultimately... That's good. So well, it feels like you are because we've answered
that, but I can be very, very clear.
No, I just wanted to clarify that. That's all.
Well, I'm pretty sure it was answered very, very
clearly. It doesn't need to be exempt because ministers have the
ability to make decisions for themselves.
And as previously, as I'm saying again now, there will be no reduction or
cuts to frontline services over the areas that you're speaking of.
That doesn't mean there won't be changes to the back office.
But ultimately, when I talk to farmers, they're very keen to make sure that the
border is secure, and they probably are happy for us to
reorganize to some degree internally in the back office, as long as
the frontline is not there. But we didn't need to go and seek an
exemption for the frontline because we're given a very, very clear
commitment over the last three years to increase
support in that area, not decrease.
Thank you, Minister. We've got a question from Dana.
Thank you. Thank you, Minister. Look, firstly, can I say congratulations on a fine
piece of work with the Indian FTA and what that will mean for our primary
production exporters
and producers. But what I'm interested in understanding more of is how is
MPI supporting those farmers, exporters, producers,
orchardists, to take advantage of the new trade agreements,
specifically India, but of course others as well.
Well, there's a couple of things there.
The first is,
so for an exporter, put the tariff reductions and quotas and so on aside for
a moment, although we've been very fortunate with our negotiators to get
things others around the world no one else has had before.
One of the more important things we can do is help exporters
from the primary sector get their goods to market a lot more quickly.
So within the free trade agreement, there are commitments on both sides, Australia,
India, and New Zealand, that perishable goods should clear their
border within 24 hours or hours, and non-perishable within
48 hours. And that would be a significant improvement from
some exporters and what they've faced over periods of time.
MPI play a very important role in that, both in ensuring
certification of the product that goes to market is recognized, but they actually
work very, very closely with their counterparts in India and other nations
to make sure the system works well and to build levels of
recognition and trust. We have people in market from
MPI, and in fact, actually, the number of, a couple of places around the
world, extra posts have been established to put more people there.
They are doing the day-to-day business of helping our
exporters
as they have products cross the border.
And they're also actually where challenges may be created, are able to
act specifically to try and work that through the system.
But maybe there's more you'd like to add.
Oh, I mean, I think we're in a pretty positive place in terms of
trade and market access around the world.
And
probably the big,
I think the challenge that's going to emerge for farmers and growers in New Zealand
is trying to fulfill all of those opportunities that exist in those trade
agreements now. And it's very diversified.
And you can see that if you look into the SOPI results.
You can see increasingly people are able to, or companies
in particular, are able to take advantage of a whole range of different markets
now. And so India's going to just give us a kind of an even bigger
diversification.
Just a supplementary on that, thank you.
Are you confident that with the increased trade, that the
certification or quality assurance at the port in terms of-
Oh
... assessing-
Yeah
... shipments or containers going out will be able to keep up?
So again,
the food safety system operates on almost
70 or 80% cost recovery. So if you're running a food business in New Zealand,
whether you're a Fonterra or a small business, it doesn't matter.
You pay for the services
that are required. So all of that certification is paid for by the companies,
and so we just adjust our staffing levels to meet the demand as is
needed. So yeah, I'm not concerned about that.
Thank you.
I've got a quick sup on that. It's an exciting opportunity for
lamb farmers as well, obviously, sheep farmers.
And I just wonder, what's this secure-- is there a secure cold chain?
Is it just for frozen? Do they just take frozen meat at this stage or?
No, they can take either there.
Or are we working on that?
And so into market,
it is very, very clear. It's the same as we do to other countries.
Then in country, that's very much to do with the exporters
and the arrangements that they have with the importers and so on.
But it's evolving very, very quickly.
Yeah.
So you look at fruit as an example, where we have had fruit go in there, although
it's been very challenging because extremely high tariff rates.
With the new FTA in place, there's a lot of talk about investment in
the cold chain there. There are New Zealand companies are looking forward to
going and playing a part in that, giving advice or help with the construction and
things like that. Some have said they want to go and establish in India
themselves, which by the way, would be an investment where New Zealanders and
Indians are employed to help grow the economy.
And so ultimately, it's a very large country.
It's a little bit like some parts of Southeast Asia have been previously.
Yeah.
This will evolve and develop over time as the amount of product that comes from New
Zealand flows and increases.
Fantastic. Thank you.
Right. Maureen.
Thank you, Madam Chair. Minister,
to show the congratulations on the work that you've
been doing for New Zealand. I want to bring my question home a little
bit, which is about the agricultural recovery assistance.
Mm.
That's more than halved in this appropriation,
and I just wondered if you've got special intel that tells you that weather
events are going to reduce, or have you got a different way
of
distributing that, and how will you target given the reduced amount?
Yeah. So
funding as it is needed is made available.
We've increased our funding in a number of areas, including in our field
days last week, announced additional funding to get people ready for weather
events. So part of it, we're also doing it as, not only just when
things happen, but how can we make sure farmers and growers are
well prepared, because there are going to be more.
And so, that investment is going into some farm leaders in areas and different
organizations and so on, and so preemptively getting them there.
But generally,
so the fund is available for us to use where we need it.
It comes from a number of places across government, but the
funds we have available to us, I think are an area that we
need.
Yeah. A quick sup?
Yes, sure.
Thank you. So if this particular fund is oversubscribed, what you're
saying is there will be money
for farmers-
Yes
... if needed.
That's right. So very much a case-by-case basis.
So we have the ability within the budget to move money around with
contingencies. A better example probably is around
biosecurity. So rather than having an amount of money,
say this is for what might happen, right, which means you can't use it
elsewhere. We have the ability to
seek additional funding within our own budget or go back to Cabinet where something
of an extreme nature has happened.
But the point that I'd make is, yes, we have the relevant funds that we need, and
should we need more, we have the ability to allocate that.
It's very reassuring. Thank you.
Mm-hmm. Thank you, Scott.
Thank you. Thank you, Minister. How much of the 2026/27 funding
allocation for the Primary Sector Growth Fund is expected to support
climate adaptation and resilience compared or
against increasing production and output?
So,
we've made some changes to the fund last year to focus and target
it more. Those who seek funding for the government have to put
more in for themselves, and we think it's the way that we can get a
better use of taxpayer money across many more projects.
It is based upon the projects that are put in, and
they are all treated equally. We have an independent panel that assesses

with the Crown, to work with landowners and foresters to find the
very best way to achieve the outcomes that we need.
I've got to say that, again, as I said earlier, I think the council has a very
challenging job because it was a significant weather event.
And there have been reports that have been done previously, previous government
that I've inherited, that draw attention to and ask questions
about the capacity and capability.
This is not a reflection necessarily on individuals in the council, it is more to
do with the significance of the scale of the event and the work that needs to be
done to rectify the land use change is a very important part of that.
I have to say that, that I am disappointed that the Gisborne Council
seem to have decided that the only real tool that they have or want to use is
that of enforcement, because there are examples of people who are
exemplary in their approach to forestry.
They are doing things they don't need to outside of their consent.
They are spending their own money and,
or inviting council out to come and talk to them about what needs to be done, and
there seems to be a fairly heavy-handed approach to enforcement.
Although, albeit, I accept enforcement is an important tool.
I think the best way to get the best outcome for Tairāwhiti is for the council to
work closely with all landowners and interested parties on an
almost a case-by-case basis on what the solutions will be there.
There are examples where people have another 10 years before they have to log, and
they've been told that actually they need to get rid of their forest
today. And ultimately, the way they've been asked to get rid of their forest, they
would view as challenging for the environment.
So I've asked officials to engage deeply with the council, provide them the support
they need. Coming back to the point of the report that was generated for the
previous government, drawing attention to the challenges
around capacity and capability of the council.
And I think it's in everybody's interest to find ways forward on that.
Mm.
Speaking of the official support,
if people think, for example, the only way out for them
to survive is carbon,
is there any support
Being provided from government to ensure we don't create future
hazards for when trees come to the end of lifecycle.
How do you mean by the only way out is carbon?
Well, if people are grappling up the coast at the moment and trying to think what
they can do, like you've said, some of them-
I know
... feel like they're going to
have to remove their forests.
For some to stay viable, they're looking at all the options.
I see.
If they decide their only option is carbon-
... what support can they expect-
... in the future?
So if you step back from support, the only reason
in the future they wouldn't have the ability to keep forest or
to have challenges on
their land is if the rules changed.
So if you think about much of the wood that's come down to weather events,
some of that is legacy wood. It is from logging many years
ago based on the rules of the time.
So we have to get the rules right. That's the reason, both with the RMA
replacement, but the changes to the
National Environment Standard for commercial forestry, we are looking at a
rules-based approach. One of the things we're saying is this is the standard for
the following reasons. Councils are able to go further than that
standard should they want, but it has to be based upon evidence.
So I think what you've seen in the past is decisions are made for a range of
reasons that probably aren't based on evidence always and,
as a result, we end up, in the future, dealing with the
consequences. So what we're trying to do across forestry, as well as agriculture
and horticulture, everything in the private sector, is to create
certainty and try and get a regulatory framework in place that is
easily understood, it's not heavy and bureaucratic, based upon risk, and achieves
what we need to. What we need in Tairāwhiti, every part of New
Zealand, is a forestry industry that is responsible, and they meet their
obligations, and that actually the rules support them.
But what we're seeing far too often, as a result of weather events, and I can
understand the reason why, is a move towards rules that actually
are prohibitive and restrictive, but in itself may not achieve what
a council would want. So the only thing I can say what support would be
available is we're going to keep working very hard with the councils, the
experts, and the forestry sector themselves, because they know as much about
growing trees as what happens on their land as anybody else does, how
we come up with the rules that are fit for purpose, and in the case of your
question, can stand the test of time.
That won't be done in one day or one week, and it'll
continue to take a lot of effort. But we actually, for
Tairāwhiti and many other parts of New Zealand, need to get the rules right,
because without forestry on land that can be forested, then
there would be a very big hole in the local economy.
And I have heard the mayor say that she wants investment in wood processing in the
area, which I agree with. But some of the things the council at the moment is
driving people away from Tairāwhiti, not towards it.
So I want to work with them to get the balance right.
Thank you.
Thank you very much, Minister.
You're welcome.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Did you have another question?
One more.
Oh, you want to ask-
